text:
Mexican gymnast Natalia Escalera showed a remarkable display of bravery after she competed with a torn ligament at theOlympicsinParis.

The 22-year-old partially tore the plantar tendon in her left foot just days before the start of the Games but refused to withdraw from the competition.

The injury prevented her from competing in all of the women’s artistic all-around gymnastics events on Sunday, however, she was cleared by Mexico’s team doctor to deliver her routine on the uneven bars.

Despite her setback, Escalera managed to finish her routine and was the Mexican with the best score, 12.800, on the apparatus.

Follow Metro.co.uk’s live blog for updates from the Paris Olympics 2024

But moments after her dismount, Escalera began to cry from the pain and required help from her coach to move away from the competition area and off the side of the stage. She was then picked up and carried to a chair.

‘I had said I wasn’t going to participate, but I had to take as many pills as I could to be able to do it and I’m very happy because I was able to fulfill my dream,’ Escalera said after making her Olympic debut.

‘They say that the stage is a painkiller, I’m in a lot of pain, but it was the last routine, so I had to give everything.

‘Every time I put my foot on the bar it was pain; I just prayed to get through the routine healthy.’

In a post on Instagram before competing, she wrote: ‘Unfortunately, in the last training session I tore the plantar tendon in my left leg where I had another tear in my calf.

‘It hurts me a lot not being able to do all around as I would have liked, sadly I was told not to compete the doctors.

‘I didn’t come this far to not be on the biggest stage in the world.

‘Only God knows why he does things but I’m here and now fighting until the end!Thank you for all your love and support towards me despite of this kind of situations.

‘It’s gymnastics and these things happen, at least not at the time we would have liked but God is giving me the opportunity today to live the experience and live my first Olympic Games even if it’s in a different way and that is to be grateful.’

Raul Carrillo, one of Mexico’s team doctors, said: ‘It was simply the aggravating factor, at the time of the start, where she had the impact due to the strong contraction of the fibres. A strong momentary pain, but she was able to support herself, thanks to the physiotherapy.

‘[She cried] because it is an impact, a strong contraction of the muscle, in this case the tendon is part of the muscle, it generates the contraction.

‘It was difficult for her to support herself at that moment. It was a partial tear of the plantar tendon, they are fibres, imagine as if it were a thread and you break some fibres.

‘The injury occurred a week ago, in the camp, when she felt the first symptoms.

Â¡Â¡Â¡Te queremos, Natalia Escalera, Te queremos!!!#ParÃ­s2024Gimnasta Mexicana Se La Rifa En Barras Pese A LesiÃ³n; Queda Fuera+ Info â¡ï¸https://t.co/ZwvgL02Vvzpic.twitter.com/Pe7gh8LSjR

‘It is one that requires rest for recovery. It depends on the size and if you can treat it, there is something called active rest, it can vary from four to six weeks.’

Escalera’s Mexican teammate, Alexa Moreno, was also making her Olympic debut in Paris and revealed she too was competing with an injury in the same event as the United States’ Simone Biles.

Moreno revealed that she had travelled to Paris with cartilage damage to her right knee. In addition to her injury, she said that her physiotherapist did not travel with the team.

‘I’m injured,’ Moreno said. ‘The physiotherapist who was with me the whole way in camp was not among the [doctors] they brought, it was a person who doesn’t really know me and doesn’t know how they bandaged me.’

After two days of the Olympics, Mexico have one medal as the trio of Alejandra Valencia, Ana Paula Vazquez and Angela Ruiz won bronze in the women’s team archery on Sunday.

title: Gymnast refuses to quit Olympics with torn ligament and ends routine crying in pain | Metro News
description: Mexican gymnast Natalia Escalera showed a remarkable display of bravery after she competed with a torn ligament at the Olympics in Paris.
